reasoning: A pandemic declaration by the WHO requires sustained human-to-human transmission spreading internationally. Current statements from the CDC, WHO, and other health officials (as of early 2025) consistently confirm that the risk to the general public remains low and there is no evidence of sustained human-to-human spread of H5N1. Historically, even after human-to-human transmission emerges, it takes the WHO about 2 to 3 months to officially declare a pandemic (e.g., March to June for H1N1 in 2009; late December to March for COVID-19). Given the July 31, 2025 deadline (only ~3.5 months away), a highly transmissible strain would have to emerge and spread almost immediately for the WHO's bureaucratic and epidemiological processes to culminate in a formal pandemic declaration before the cutoff. The combination of a very short time horizon, historical WHO timeline delays, and the current absence of any human-to-human clusters makes this event extremely unlikely.
